text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac widely, then dissects along the lower border of the pancreas and the mass. A large cystic mucinocystic tumor replaces the body and tail of the pancreas, adherent to the transverse muscle column, proximal jejunal loops, and duodenal flexure. The tumor is carefully dissected off these structures, leaving a portion of mesocolon attached to the cyst to prevent rupture and spillage.
The surgeon continues dissection inferiorly towards the retroperitoneum, identifying and separating the loose areolar tissue plate within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon identifies the junction of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein while dissecting medially. The lesser sac is further opened towards the duodenum to enhance the surgical view.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the neck of the pancreas, focusing on the superior mesenteric portal vein axis. The splenic vein, stretched and arched due to the size of the cyst, is identified with small tributaries draining into it. Clips are meticulously applied to the short stem of the splenic vein at its junction with the portal vein, and the vein is divided.
The surgeon divides the neck of the pancreas due to the large cyst, which obscures the splenic artery. After division, the splenic artery is identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon performs further dissection towards the body and tail of the pancreas in the retroperitoneum, separating the spleen and the entire specimen from surrounding tissues. The short gastric vessels are divided, and the specimen is placed into a surgical bag.
The surgeon places small fine stitches to secure the surgical site before delivering the specimen from the operative field.
